title: Networking: The Universal Attack Surface
description: Why every cyber attack starts or ends with a network packet.

slides.title: The Attack Surface 🎯
slides.content:
# What is an Attack Surface?

In cybersecurity, the **Attack Surface** is the sum of all the points where an unauthorized user (the "attacker") can try to enter data into or extract data from an environment.

Think of a **Castle**:
- The **Physical Walls** are one attack surface (can they climb it?).
- The **Front Gate** is one (can they ram it down?).
- The **Secret Tunnel** is one (does anyone know about it?).
- The **Pigeon Roost** is one (can they send a fake message?).

In the digital world, **Networking is the front gate, the secret tunnel, and the pigeon roost all rolled into one.**

slides.title: Why is the Network the Weakest Link? 🔗
slides.content:
# Inherent Trust

Here is a hard truth about how the internet was built: **It was built on trust.**

When the early internet protocols (TCP/IP) were designed in the 70s and 80s, it was a small club of researchers and military personnel. They trusted each other. They didn't need encryption or authentication baked into every packet.

**Analogy:** It's like a small town where everyone leaves their doors unlocked and keys in the car ignition. Suddenly, that town becomes New York City. The **infrastructure** (roads/network protocols) hasn't changed, but the **threats** have multiplied.

Because of this legacy design, network traffic can be:
- **Eavesdropped** (Sniffed)
- **Impersonated** (Spoofed)
- **Intercepted** (Man-in-the-Middle)

quizData.question: Why is the network considered a 'universal attack surface'?

options.text: Because it is made of physical cables.
Because every attack involves network traffic at some point.

quizData.explanation: Exactly. Even if malware is delivered by USB (physical), it usually needs to 'phone home' over the network to exfiltrate data.

slides.title: Visualizing a Packet 📦
slides.content:
Everything on a network is sent in **Packets**. Think of a packet like a postcard. Everyone handling it can read it unless you put it in an envelope (Encryption).

Here is a representation of what a packet looks like in transit:
slides.codeSnippet:
--- IP Packet ---
| Source IP:     192.168.1.10 |
| Destination IP: 8.8.8.8       |
| Data:          'Request: google.com' |
--- End ---

slides.title: The Postcard Problem 📬
slides.content:
## Visibility

In the early days of networking (using **Hubs**), when Computer A sent a packet to Computer B, **EVERY** other computer on the network also received that postcard. It was up to the computer's network card to say, "Oh, that's not for me," and throw it away.

A hacker with a **Packet Sniffer** (like Wireshark) simply tells their computer: *"Don't throw away those postcards. Read them all to me."*

This is why we use **Switches** (which direct traffic specifically) and **Encryption** (which puts the postcard in an opaque envelope).
